title: European Figure Skating Championships

infobox.value: are an annual [figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU). They are figure skating's oldest competition. The first European Championships were held in 1891 in [Hamburg, Germany](/wiki/Hamburg,_Germany "Hamburg, Germany"), and featured only one segment – [compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures") – with seven competitors. They have only been interrupted five times since 1891. Women were allowed to compete for the first time in 1930, which was also when [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") was added to the competition. [Ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") was added in 1954. Only eligible skaters from ISU member countries in Europe are allowed to compete, while skaters from countries outside of Europe instead compete at the [Four Continents Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "Four Continents Figure Skating Championships").

The gold, silver, and bronze medalists in the men's event at the [2016 European Championships](/wiki/2016_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2016 European Figure Skating Championships"): [Javier Fernández](/wiki/Javier_Fern%C3%A1ndez_(figure_skater) "Javier Fernández (figure skater)") of Spain *(center)*, [Alexei Bychenko](/wiki/Alexei_Bychenko "Alexei Bychenko") of Israel *(left)*, and [Maxim Kovtun](/wiki/Maxim_Kovtun "Maxim Kovtun") of Russia *(right)*

[![The women's medalists at the 2010 European Championships](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/2010_EC_Ladies_Podium.jpg/250px-2010_EC_Ladies_Podium.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010_EC_Ladies_Podium.jpg)

The gold, silver, and bronze medalists in the women's event at the [2010 European Championships](/wiki/2010_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2010 European Figure Skating Championships"): [Carolina Kostner](/wiki/Carolina_Kostner "Carolina Kostner") of Italy *(center)*, [Laura Lepistö](/wiki/Laura_Lepist%C3%B6 "Laura Lepistö") of Finland *(left)*, and [Elene Gedevanishvili](/wiki/Elene_Gedevanishvili "Elene Gedevanishvili") of Georgia *(right)*

[![The pairs medalists at the 2012 European Championships](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/2012_European_FSC_-_Pairs.jpg/250px-2012_European_FSC_-_Pairs.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_European_FSC_-_Pairs.jpg)

The gold, silver, and bronze medalists in the pairs event at the [2012 European Championships](/wiki/2012_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2012 European Figure Skating Championships"): [Tatiana Volosozhar](/wiki/Tatiana_Volosozhar "Tatiana Volosozhar") and [Maxim Trankov](/wiki/Maxim_Trankov "Maxim Trankov") of Russia *(center)*, [Vera Bazarova](/wiki/Vera_Bazarova "Vera Bazarova") and [Yuri Larionov](/wiki/Yuri_Larionov "Yuri Larionov") of Russia *(left)*, and [Ksenia Stolbova](/wiki/Ksenia_Stolbova "Ksenia Stolbova") and [Fedor Klimov](/wiki/Fedor_Klimov "Fedor Klimov") of Russia *(right)*

[![The ice dance medalists at the 2014 European Championships](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/2014_European_Championships_Dance_Podium.jpg/250px-2014_European_Championships_Dance_Podium.jpg)](/wiki/File:2014_European_Championships_Dance_Podium.jpg)

The gold, silver, and bronze medalists in the ice dance event at the [2014 European Championships](/wiki/2014_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2014 European Figure Skating Championships"): [Anna Cappellini](/wiki/Anna_Cappellini "Anna Cappellini") and [Luca Lanotte](/wiki/Luca_Lanotte "Luca Lanotte") of Italy *(center)*, [Elena Ilinykh](/wiki/Elena_Ilinykh "Elena Ilinykh") and [Nikita Katsalapov](/wiki/Nikita_Katsalapov "Nikita Katsalapov") of Russia *(left)*, and [Penny Coomes](/wiki/Penny_Coomes "Penny Coomes") and [Nicholas Buckland](/wiki/Nicholas_Buckland "Nicholas Buckland") of Great Britain *(right)*

The **European Figure Skating Championships** are an annual [figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU). They are figure skating's oldest competition. The first European Championships were held in 1891 in [Hamburg, Germany](/wiki/Hamburg,_Germany "Hamburg, Germany"), and featured only one segment – [compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures") – with seven competitors. They have only been interrupted five times since 1891. Women were allowed to compete for the first time in 1930, which was also when [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") was added to the competition. [Ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") was added in 1954. Only eligible skaters from ISU member countries in Europe are allowed to compete, while skaters from countries outside of Europe instead compete at the [Four Continents Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "Four Continents Figure Skating Championships").

Medals are awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), pair skating, and ice dance. [Ulrich Salchow](/wiki/Ulrich_Salchow "Ulrich Salchow") of Sweden holds the record for winning the most European Championship titles in men's singles (with nine), while [Irina Slutskaya](/wiki/Irina_Slutskaya "Irina Slutskaya") of Russia holds the record in women's singles (with seven). [Irina Rodnina](/wiki/Irina_Rodnina "Irina Rodnina") and [Alexander Zaitsev](/wiki/Alexander_Zaitsev_(pair_skater) "Alexander Zaitsev (pair skater)") of the Soviet Union hold the record in pair skating (with seven), although Rodnina won an additional four titles with a previous partner. [Lyudmila Pakhomova](/wiki/Lyudmila_Pakhomova "Lyudmila Pakhomova") and [Aleksandr Gorshkov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Gorshkov_(figure_skater) "Aleksandr Gorshkov (figure skater)") of the Soviet Union hold the record in ice dance (with six), while [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") of France has also won six titles in ice dance, but not with the same partner.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Although they have not been held continuously, the European Championships are the oldest championship competition in [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating"). The first European Championships were held in [1891](/wiki/1891_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1891 European Figure Skating Championships") in [Hamburg, Germany](/wiki/Hamburg,_Germany "Hamburg, Germany"), and featured only one segment: [compulsory figures](/wiki/Compulsory_figures "Compulsory figures").[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) The event was sponsored by the skating federations of Austria and Germany after they had combined to become one federation.[[2]](#cite_note-2) All of the medalists were from Germany: [Oskar Uhlig](/wiki/Oskar_Uhlig "Oskar Uhlig") won the first gold medal, [A. Schmitson](/wiki/A._Schmitson "A. Schmitson") finished in second place, and [Franz Zilly](/wiki/Franz_Zilly "Franz Zilly") was third.[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1)

The [1893 European Championships](/wiki/1893_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1893 European Figure Skating Championships") were the first to be held under the jurisdiction of the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), which had formed in the summer of 1892. The championships were sponsored by the Berlin Skating Club and organized by the German/Austrian federation. Figure skating historian James Hines called the 1893 European Championships "clearly a success from a skating standpoint",[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) but it also marked figure skating's "first major controversy", due to "different interpretations of the scoring rules, which could result in a tie depending upon one's interpretation of them".[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) The Berlin Skating Club had declared [Henning Grenander](/wiki/Henning_Grenander "Henning Grenander") the winner, but the ISU declared [Eduard Engelmann Jr.](/wiki/Eduard_Engelmann_Jr. "Eduard Engelmann Jr.") of Austria the winner.[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) According to Hines, the discrepancy in scoring was due to the interpretation of the scoring rules, resulting in a possible tie.[[3]](#cite_note-hines2011-63-3) The problem was never resolved, but in 1895, the ISU declared the 1893 results invalid and rules were established that made sure that discrepancies due to differences in scoring interpretations could not occur again.[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1)[[3]](#cite_note-hines2011-63-3) ISU historian Benjamin T. Wright said that the controversy "nearly led to the demise" of the newly formed ISU.[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1)

The next two European Championships, [1894](/wiki/1894_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1894 European Figure Skating Championships") and [1895](/wiki/1893_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1893 European Figure Skating Championships"), "experienced a marked decrease in participation, perhaps a result of the scoring debacle".[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) There were no European Championships for two years, which Hines speculated was due to the small number of contestants in 1894 and 1895, although the competition returned in [1898](/wiki/1898_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1898 European Figure Skating Championships").[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) Hines also reported that the European Championships were cancelled in 1902 and 1903 "for lack of ice".[[4]](#cite_note-Hines2011-4) By the beginning of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), twenty European Championships had been held. There were three more interruptions of the European Championships: between 1915 and 1922 due to World War I, between 1940 and 1946 due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"),[[4]](#cite_note-Hines2011-4) and in [2021](/wiki/2021_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 European Figure Skating Championships") due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Only men competed at the European Championships until 1930, when [women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating") and [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") were added. Members of all ISU nations, not just those from Europe, were allowed to compete at the Europeans Championships until 1948.[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) After [Barbara Ann Scott](/wiki/Barbara_Ann_Scott "Barbara Ann Scott") of Canada and [Dick Button](/wiki/Dick_Button "Dick Button") of the United States won the [1948 European Championships](/wiki/1948_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "1948 European Figure Skating Championships"), while [Eva Pawlik](/wiki/Eva_Pawlik "Eva Pawlik") of Austria and [Hans Gerschwiler](/wiki/Hans_Gerschwiler "Hans Gerschwiler") of Switzerland were awarded the silver medals, the competition was restricted to European skaters.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[1]](#cite_note-Hines2015-1) [Ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") was added in 1954.[[4]](#cite_note-Hines2011-4) Competitions were held in outdoor rinks until 1967, when the ISU ruled that both the European and [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") had to be held in covered ice rinks.[[4]](#cite_note-Hines2011-4)

The [2027 European Championships](/wiki/2027_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2027 European Figure Skating Championships") are scheduled to be held from 25 to 31 January in [Lausanne, Switzerland](/wiki/Lausanne,_Switzerland "Lausanne, Switzerland").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Qualifying

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Qualifying")]

Only competitors who are "members of a European ISU Member" are eligible to compete in the European Championships.[[9]](#cite_note-SPandID-9) Each ISU member country can submit at least one competitor per discipline, with a maximum of three competitors per discipline if they have earned the minimum total element scores, which is determined and published each season by the ISU, during the current or immediately previous season.[[9]](#cite_note-SPandID-9)

Age restrictions have changed throughout the history of the European Championships. Until the [2023–24 figure skating season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_figure_skating_season "2023–24 figure skating season"), skaters had to be at least 15 years old before 1 July of the previous year.[[10]](#cite_note-espn-10) At the [ISU Congress](/wiki/International_Skating_Union#ISU_Congress "International Skating Union") held in June 2022, members of the [ISU Council](/wiki/International_Skating_Union#ISU_Council "International Skating Union") accepted a proposal to gradually increase the minimum age limit for senior competition to 17 beginning with the 2024–25 season. To avoid forcing skaters who had already competed in the senior category to return to juniors, the age limit remained unchanged during the 2022–23 season, before increasing to 16 during the 2023–24 season, and then to 17 during the 2024–25 season.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Women's singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Women's singles")]

On 29 January 2024, the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") disqualified [Kamila Valieva](/wiki/Kamila_Valieva "Kamila Valieva") of Russia for four years retroactive to 25 December 2021 for an anti-doping violation.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Thus, her scores from the [2022 European Championships](/wiki/2022_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 European Figure Skating Championships") were annulled.[[40]](#cite_note-41) As a result, [Anna Shcherbakova](/wiki/Anna_Shcherbakova "Anna Shcherbakova") of Russia was elevated to gold medalist, [Alexandra Trusova](/wiki/Alexandra_Trusova "Alexandra Trusova") of Russia to silver, and [Loena Hendrickx](/wiki/Loena_Hendrickx "Loena Hendrickx") of Belgium to bronze, breaking what had otherwise been an all-Russian sweep of the women's podium that year.[[41]](#cite_note-ISUReranking-42)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Records")]

[![Ulrich Salchow at the 1908 Olympic Games](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg/250px-1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg)](/wiki/File:1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg)

[![Irina Slutskaya competes in the women's short program at the 2002 Olympics](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Irina_Slutskaya_2002_Olympics_Short_Program_01.jpg/250px-Irina_Slutskaya_2002_Olympics_Short_Program_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irina_Slutskaya_2002_Olympics_Short_Program_01.jpg)

[![Irina Rodrina and Alexei Ulanov in 1972](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Rodnina_and_Ulanov_1972.jpg/250px-Rodnina_and_Ulanov_1972.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodnina_and_Ulanov_1972.jpg)

[![Lyudmila Pakhomova and Aleksandr Gorshkov performing on ice](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H1219-0016-001%2C_Ludmilla_Pachomowa%2C_Alexander_Gorschkow.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H1219-0016-001%2C_Ludmilla_Pachomowa%2C_Alexander_Gorschkow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H1219-0016-001,_Ludmilla_Pachomowa,_Alexander_Gorschkow.jpg)

[![Gabriella Papadakis and Guillaume Cizeron at the 2019 Internationaux de France](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Papadakis%2C_Cizeron_-_2019_Internationaux_de_France_-_4.jpg/250px-Papadakis%2C_Cizeron_-_2019_Internationaux_de_France_-_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papadakis,_Cizeron_-_2019_Internationaux_de_France_-_4.jpg)

*From left to right:* [Ulrich Salchow](/wiki/Ulrich_Salchow "Ulrich Salchow") of Sweden won nine European Championship titles in [men's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"); [Irina Slutskaya](/wiki/Irina_Slutskaya "Irina Slutskaya") of Russia won seven European Championship titles in [women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"); [Irina Rodnina](/wiki/Irina_Rodnina "Irina Rodnina") of the Soviet Union won eleven European Championship titles in [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), four of which were with [Alexei Ulanov](/wiki/Alexei_Ulanov "Alexei Ulanov"); [Lyudmila Pakhomova](/wiki/Lyudmila_Pakhomova "Lyudmila Pakhomova") and [Aleksandr Gorshkov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Gorshkov_(figure_skater) "Aleksandr Gorshkov (figure skater)") of the Soviet Union won six European Championship titles in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); and [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") of France also won six European Championship titles in ice dance, five of which were with [Gabriella Papadakis](/wiki/Gabriella_Papadakis "Gabriella Papadakis").
